en.extract:
The pale-bellied white-eye (Zosterops consobrinorum) is a species of bird in the family Zosteropidae. It is endemic to SE Sulawesi in Indonesia. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest. The species has a limited distribution  and also appears to be sensitive to human disturbance.


Taxonomy and systematics
Although not yet recognized as a subspecies of the pale-bellied white-eye, in 2003 a similar bird was discovered near the village of Wanci on Wangi-wangi Island and provisionally named the Wangi-Wangi white-eye (Zosterops sp. nov.). This island is its only known home and is part of the Tukangbesi Islands to the southeast of Sulawesi in Indonesia.
The Wangi-Wangi white-eye has a number of differences from the pale-bellied white-eye including larger size, a black body, a long yellow beak, a gray breast and pale feet. Like most white-eyes it has a white ring around the eye and green upperparts.

The '''pale-bellied white-eye''' ('''''Zosterops consobrinorum''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Zosteropidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to SE [[Sulawesi]] in [[Indonesia]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]]. The species has a limited distribution <ref>{{Cite book|title=Family Zosteropidae (White-eyes). In: del Hoyo J, Elliot A, Christie DA, editors Handbook of the Birds of the World. Vol. 13. Penduline-tits to Shrikes.|last=van Balen|first=S|publisher=Lynx Edicions|year=2008|isbn=978-84-96553-45-3|location=Barcelona|pages=402–485}}</ref> and also appears to be sensitive to human disturbance.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Martin|first1=Thomas Edward|last2=Blackburn|first2=George Alan|date=2010|title=Impacts of Tropical Forest Disturbance Upon Avifauna on a Small Island with High Endemism: Implications for Conservation|journal=Conservation & Society|volume=8|issue=2|pages=127–139|doi=10.4103/0972-4923.68914|doi-access=free|hdl=10535/6336|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
Although not yet recognized as a subspecies of the pale-bellied white-eye, in 2003 a similar bird was discovered near the village of Wanci on [[Wangi-wangi Island]] and provisionally named the '''Wangi-Wangi white-eye''' (''[[Zosterops]] sp. nov.'').<ref name="NatGeo" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=O'Connell|first1=Darren P|last2=Kelly|first2=David J|last3=Lawless|first3=Naomi|last4=O'Brien|first4=Katie|last5=Marcaigh|first5=Fionn Ó|last6=Karya|first6=Adi|last7=Analuddin|first7=Kangkuso|last8=Marples|first8=Nicola M|date=2019|title=A sympatric pair of undescribed white-eye species (Aves: Zosteropidae: Zosterops) with different origins|journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society|language=en|volume=186|issue=3|pages=701–724|doi=10.1093/zoolinnean/zlz022|issn=0024-4082|doi-access=free}}</ref> This island is its only known home and is part of the [[Tukangbesi Islands]] to the southeast of [[Sulawesi]] in [[Indonesia]].

The Wangi-Wangi white-eye has a number of differences from the pale-bellied white-eye including larger size, a black body, a long yellow beak, a gray breast and pale feet. Like most white-eyes it has a white ring around the eye and green upperparts.
